content:
The tools cost you about $100 a month per client, and the math is simple. Landing the clients is the part nobody prices. Here is the real breakdown.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Can You Make $5,000 a Month Selling AI Phone Agents? (AI Generated Image)It is 6:14 on a Tuesday evening. A guy who runs a two-truck HVAC shop is up a ladder finishing a job. His phone buzzes in the truck below him, three rings, then voicemail. The caller — a homeowner with a dead AC unit and a hot house — hangs up without leaving a message and dials the next company on the list. That call was worth about $1,500. He will never know it happened.
Here is the number that turns that scene into a business: roughly 62% of calls to small businesses go unanswered, and about 85% of the people who don’t get through never call back. They call a competitor instead. An AI phone agent — software that answers the phone, sounds human, books the job, and texts the owner — catches those calls. And setting them up for local shops has become one of the most-hyped money plays of 2026. So I priced the whole thing, top to bottom: what it costs you to run, what you can charge, and what it really takes to hit $5,000 a month.
The thing you are actually selling is a missed call
Before any tool talk, understand the pitch, because the pitch is the whole business. Local service businesses — dentists, plumbers, HVAC, law offices, med spas, salons — live and die on the phone, and they miss a shocking share of it.
The published numbers are brutal. Only about 38% of business calls get answered; the other 62% go to voicemail or nowhere. Of the people who hit voicemail, roughly 85% never call back, and about 62% go straight to a competitor. Speed decides it: around 78% of callers buy from whoever picks up first, not whoever is best or cheapest.
Put a dollar figure on it and the owner leans in. A dental practice that misses ten new-patient calls a month, at an average patient worth around $800, is bleeding close to $96,000 a year. A contractor loses $800 to $1,500 a month to calls that ring out. Each individual missed call is worth somewhere around $100 to $200 on average.
You are not selling “AI.” You are selling the calls they are already losing. That framing is the difference between a blank stare and a signed client.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why a missed call is a lost customer, not a callback.What an AI phone agent actually is (in plain words)
Strip the jargon. An AI phone agent is a piece of software that answers a real phone number, talks to the caller in a natural voice, and does a few useful jobs: answer common questions (hours, pricing, “do you take my insurance”), book or reschedule an appointment on the calendar, take a message, and text or email the owner the moment something urgent comes in. It runs 24/7 and it never puts anyone on hold.
You build it on a platform — Retell, Vapi, and a handful of others — that stitches together the voice, the language model, and the phone line. You do not write the AI from scratch. You configure it: write what it should say, connect it to the client’s calendar and phone number, and test it until it stops sounding like a robot. That configuration work, and the ongoing babysitting, is what the client pays you for.
What it costs you to run one (the real per-minute receipt)
This is where the hype articles wave their hands. The actual costs are small, knowable, and worth memorizing, because your margin lives here.
The voice platform is billed by the minute. Retell runs about $0.07 a minute. Vapi starts near $0.05 a minute for the raw platform, but once you add a real speech engine, a language model, and a phone line, a basic stack lands around $0.14 to $0.15 a minute, and a premium setup (a top-tier voice plus a frontier model) climbs to $0.25 to $0.33. The industry rule of thumb for an all-in, production voice minute in 2026 is about $0.10 to $0.30.
The phone line is separate and cheap. A local number on Twilio rents for about $1.15 a month, and inbound minutes to it cost roughly $0.0085 each. Call recording, if you want it, adds a fraction of a cent.
Now do the math for one real client. Say a busy shop takes 250 calls a month and the average call runs two and a half minutes. That is about 625 talk-minutes. At a mid-range $0.12 a minute all-in, that is about $75 in usage. Add the phone number and inbound minutes, call it another $6. Add your slice of the platform’s base subscription spread across clients, maybe $20 to $50. You are into that client for somewhere around $100 to $150 a month, all in.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The full cost stack to run one AI phone agent for a busy local shop.What you can charge — and the margin that is left
Here is the good news the cost table sets up. Agencies price this service far above what it costs to run.
Real 2026 pricing looks like this. A one-time setup or build fee usually runs $500 to $3,000 for a small business, and climbs past $25,000 only for complex enterprise work. The monthly retainer — which covers hosting, monitoring, tweaks, and fixes — commonly runs $297 to $997 a month on white-label platforms, and $500 to $3,000 for more involved voice work. Agencies report keeping 50% to 70% of that as profit, because their platform bill per client sits down around $99 to $299.
Take a deliberately conservative package so the numbers are honest: $500 to set it up, $500 a month to run it. Your real cost is that $100 to $150. That leaves roughly $350 to $400 a month in profit per client, a margin near 70%, plus the setup fee up front. That is not a hype number. It is what is left after you pay every bill above.
The $5,000-a-month math (and why it is not passive)
Now the headline. At $500 a month per client, $5,000 a month is ten retained clients. Ten. After costs, that is roughly $3,750 in your pocket every month, before the setup fees you collect on top each time you land a new one.
Ten clients is completely doable. It is also completely not passive, and this is where I have to be straight with you. The software is the easy 20% of this business. You can stand up a working agent in an afternoon. The other 80% — the part that actually pays — is sales and support. You have to find ten local business owners, get past their skepticism, close them, and then keep the agent working well enough that they don’t cancel. Miss that, and your $5,000 spreadsheet stays a spreadsheet.
Most people who try this land zero to two clients in their first month. Not because the tech failed. Because selling to local businesses is a grind of walk-ins, follow-ups, and demos, and most first-timers quit before the tenth “not interested.”
Who actually makes this work
The people who hit real numbers do three things differently, and none of them are secret.
They pick one niche and own it. “AI for businesses” closes no one. “I set up a phone assistant for dental offices that books new patients while your front desk is slammed” closes. One vertical means one script, one demo, one set of objections you learn cold.
They lead with the missed-call money, not the technology. The line that signs clients is some version of: this pays for itself if it saves you one job a month. A $500 retainer against one $800 dental patient or one $1,500 HVAC call is an easy yes, and it is true — which is why it works.
And they treat it as outbound sales from day one. The agencies quietly reporting those 50-to-70% margins are not waiting for inbound leads. They are recording a demo agent answering a fake call for their niche, then putting it in front of five owners a day until one says yes.
The honest verdict, and your first step
So, can you make $5,000 a month selling AI phone agents? Yes, and the margins are real — around 70% on a modest package, backed by costs you can verify to the cent. This is one of the few 2026 money plays where the receipt holds up.
But it works for a specific person: someone willing to do sales, pick one boring vertical, and support a client after the sale. It is a service business wearing an AI costume, not a passive-income button. If cold outreach makes you want to close the laptop, this is not your play, and that is worth knowing before you spend a weekend on it.
If it is your play, here is the first step, and it is free. Spin up one agent on a platform with a free tier — Vapi gives you 1,000 free minutes — and point it at a single niche you can name. Record a two-minute demo of it answering a realistic call for that niche. Then take it to five businesses of that exact type this week and ask one question: “How many calls do you think you miss after hours?” Let the missed-call math do the selling. Close one at $500 to set up and $500 a month. Then do it nine more times.
What would you charge for your first client — and which local niche would you go after? Drop your number below; I want to see the math you would run.
